research.title: Testing Next.js 16 for Real: Vitest, Playwright, and Server Actions
research.whyTrending: The blog has deep Next.js coverage but almost nothing on testing. RSC + Server Actions broke old testing assumptions and there's high search intent for how to unit-test actions and E2E-test App Router flows in 2026.

seo.suggestedTitle: Testing Next.js 16: Vitest, Playwright & Server Actions

gaps: Most tutorials do not clearly draw the Vitest/Playwright BOUNDARY (what goes where and why)
Async server component limitation stated but the WHY (async execution env) rarely explained
Server action testing shown as toy; not the auth-branch matrix
No mention that server is the test boundary for E2E (dont mock actions)
strengths: Aurora Scharff post strong on RSC async testing

officialSources.note: official Vitest setup; async RSC unsupported
Playwright E2E, test against production build
how to unit test server actions - community patterns

expertInsights: Two layers: Vitest+RTL for server actions/zod/sync components; Playwright for async RSC + full flows
Vitest cannot render async Server Components - the render throws on await; use E2E instead
cookies()/headers() need request-scoped async context absent in tests - mock next/headers
Server actions unit-tested as plain functions; mock redirect() from next/navigation
E2E: server is the test boundary - dont try to mock server actions, drive the UI
Playwright: test against production build, fresh browser contexts, role-based selectors, POM
commonProblems: Trying to render an async RSC in Vitest and fighting the throw
Mocking server actions in E2E instead of driving real flow
Testing dev server instead of prod build (flaky)

strategy.angle: A boundary-first testing guide: the single most useful thing is knowing WHAT to test with Vitest vs Playwright and WHY (async RSC is the dividing line). Give the real setup, the mocking gotchas (next/headers, redirect), a server-action auth-branch matrix, and the E2E principle that the server is the test boundary
strategy.title: Testing Next.js 16: Where Vitest Ends and Playwright Begins
strategy.excerpt: The hardest part of testing Next.js 16 is not writing tests, it is knowing which tool tests what. Async Server Components draw a hard line: Vitest cannot render them, Playwright must. Here is the boundary, the mocking gotchas, and the server-action tests that actually catch bugs.
hooks: Contrarian: the async Server Component you cannot unit test is telling you where your E2E boundary is
Gotcha: the moment you add await to a Server Component, Vitest throws - and that is by design
Practical: server actions are just functions - test the auth branches, dont mock the framework
outline: Hook: the test that threw the moment I added await
The two-layer model and the line between them
Layer 1: Vitest for server actions and sync components
Mocking the framework: next/headers and next/navigation
The server-action test matrix that catches real bugs
Why async Server Components break Vitest
Layer 2: Playwright, where the server is the boundary
Dont mock server actions in E2E
